summary.brief: Land v. Whitley. Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: Mrs. Land was diagnosed with a high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion in early 2020, which was at high risk of turning into cervical cancer.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On 29 June 2020, Defendant Dr. Whitley, assisted by resident-in-training Dr. Faiz, performed a TVH on Mrs. Land at Vidant SurgiCenter.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Whitley noted in the operative notes that due to Mrs. Land’s anatomy she had difficulty during the procedure.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Despite these complications, Dr. Whitley did not convert the vaginal hysterectomy to an abdominal or laparoscopic procedure.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Consequently, a three-inch piece of uterine tissue remained undetected in her abdominal cavity following the TVH surgery.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On 14 July 2020, Mrs. Land attended a routine post-operative visit with Dr. Whitley during which she reported experiencing abdominal pain.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Whitley noted in her medical record that Mrs. Land had no complaints other than 'struggling with constipation'.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On 25 July 2020, Mrs. Land presented with severe abdominal pain to Vidant Emergency Department in Greenville where she was diagnosed with sepsis, stage 4 kidney failure, and an abdominal infection.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. McDonald removed approximately twelve inches of Mrs. Land’s bowel and left the wound open in order to drain the infection.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Mrs. Land was finally discharged from Vidant Hospital on 7 August 2020.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: During recovery, Mrs. Land developed pulmonary emboli in both of her lungs, and she was hospitalized again because of complications from the infected uterine remnant.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On 16 February 2022, Plaintiffs, Mrs. Land and her husband, filed a complaint against Defendants Dr. Whitley, Greenville OB/GYN, Vidant Medical Center, and Vidant SurgiCenter.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Because Plaintiffs’ complaint, liberally construed, sufficiently alleges claims not barred by N.C. Gen. Stat. § 90-21.133(a), Defendants are not entitled to dismissal of Plaintiffs’ gross negligence claims at this stage of litigation.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: We affirm the trial court’s order denying Defendants’ motions to dismiss pursuant to Rule 12(b)(2), Rule 12(b)(6), and Rule 9(j).
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Mrs. Land presented with severe abdominal pain to Vidant Emergency Department in Greenville on 25 July 2020.

focused_exam.abdomen: Severe abdominal pain, findings not detailed in judgment

initial_state.initial_working_problem: Severe abdominal pain with suspected infection

reveal.clinical_significance: Indicates infection and need for further surgical intervention

timeline_phases.name: Emergency Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to ED with severe abdominal pain

timeline_phases.name: Post-operative Follow-up
timeline_phases.description: Routine post-operative visit with Dr. Whitley

key_events.event: Post-operative visit with reported abdominal pain

decision_points.clinical_context: During TVH, difficulty encountered due to anatomy
decision_points.prompt: What should be done when encountering difficulty during TVH?

options.description: Convert to abdominal or laparoscopic procedure
options.clinical_reasoning: Allows better visualization and complete removal of uterine tissue

options.description: Continue with vaginal hysterectomy despite difficulty
options.clinical_reasoning: Limited visualization, risk of incomplete procedure

expected_action_court.description: Conversion to abdominal or laparoscopic procedure should have been done
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence established conversion is standard when visualization is inadequate

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to convert led to retained uterine tissue

decision_points.clinical_context: Post-operative visit with reported abdominal pain
decision_points.prompt: What should be done during post-operative visit when patient reports abdominal pain?

options.description: Conduct thorough physical examination and consider imaging
options.clinical_reasoning: Necessary to identify potential complications or infections

options.description: Renew pain medication without further examination

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant renewed pain medication without further examination

expected_action_court.description: Thorough examination and imaging should have been conducted
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Standard practice to investigate reported pain post-surgery

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to examine led to missed diagnosis of infection
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Examination and imaging to identify infection

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed severe complications including sepsis and pulmonary emboli
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Chronic pain, inability to lift, memory loss, mood disturbances

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to convert procedure and inadequate post-operative care
Causation established - retained uterine tissue led to severe infection

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to convert procedure and inadequate follow-up
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Conversion and proper follow-up would have prevented complications

diagnoses.diagnosis: Sepsis, stage 4 kidney failure, abdominal infection

allegations.allegation: Failure to convert procedure and inadequate follow-up
